questions.question: Identify and briefly describe three key hardware components typically found on an IoT development board.
keyPoints: Microcontroller or Microprocessor (MCU/MPU): The central computing unit that executes program instructions and processes data.
Communication Module: Hardware (e.g., Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, LoRa, cellular modem) that enables the board to connect to networks and other devices.
General-Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) Pins: Physical interfaces that allow the board to sense inputs from and control outputs to the external world (e.g., sensors, actuators).

questions.question: Explain the importance of the microcontroller unit (MCU) in an IoT device. Discuss three key factors to consider when selecting an MCU for a specific IoT application.
keyPoints: The MCU is the 'brain' of the IoT device, responsible for executing the firmware, processing sensor data, managing power, and controlling communication protocols.
Key selection factors include: 1) Processing Power & Clock Speed: Must be sufficient to handle the application's computational demands and real-time requirements.
2) Power Consumption: Critical for battery-operated devices; low-power states (sleep, deep sleep) are essential for longevity.
3) Integrated Peripherals & Memory: On-chip features like ADCs, DACs, communication interfaces (I2C, SPI, UART), and sufficient flash/RAM reduce external component count and cost.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the roles of a microcontroller (MCU) and a microprocessor (MPU) in the context of IoT device design. Discuss the typical use cases, architectural differences, and trade-offs (e.g., power, performance, cost) that influence the choice between them for an IoT product.
keyPoints: Architectural Difference: An MCU integrates a processor core, memory (RAM/Flash), and programmable peripherals (I2C, SPI, UART, ADC) on a single chip (System-on-a-Chip). An MPU is a standalone CPU requiring external memory chips and peripherals.

Use Cases & Trade-offs: MCUs are used for resource-constrained, low-power, cost-sensitive applications (e.g., sensor nodes, smart tags) due to their low power consumption, integrated design, and lower cost. MPUs are used for compute-intensive applications requiring high performance and running complex operating systems (e.g., Linux) (e.g., smart gateways, edge analytics devices), but at the cost of higher power consumption and a more complex, expensive board design.

The choice hinges on the application's need for processing capability versus power budget and unit cost. An MCU is optimal for deeply embedded control, while an MPU is chosen for data-heavy processing and user interfaces.

questions.question: Compare Arduino, Raspberry Pi, and ESP32 boards for IoT applications, discussing their strengths and ideal use cases.
keyPoints: Arduino: MCU-based, simple real-time control, low power, ideal for sensor nodes
Raspberry Pi: MPU with full Linux OS, high compute, ideal as gateway or edge device
ESP32: MCU with built-in WiFi+BT, low cost, ideal for wireless IoT endpoints
Selection depends on processing needs, power budget, connectivity, and cost
